Good morning,

Today=E2=80=99s Stratechery Interview =E2=80=94 and the last Interview of t=
he summer =E2=80=94 is with Marc Andreessen. Andreessen hardly needs an int=
roduction: while at the University of Illinois Andreessen co-created the Mo=
saic web browser, which became the foundation of Netscape, the defining Int=
ernet 1.0 company. Today Andreessen is best known for being the co-founder =
of the Andreessen Horowitz venture capital firm, a disruptive entity in its=
 own right, with its services approach to venture and massive fund sizes. W=
hat Andreessen is arguably best known for, though, is being a public intell=
ectual of sorts. In this role Andreessen has authored three seminal essays:=
 Why Software Is Eating the World ( https://a16z.com/2011/08/20/why-softwar=
e-is-eating-the-world/ ) , It=E2=80=99s Time to Build ( https://a16z.com/20=
20/04/18/its-time-to-build/ ) , and just a few weeks ago, Why AI Will Save =
the World ( https://a16z.com/2023/06/06/ai-will-save-the-world/ ).

In this extensive interview, recorded last week, we discuss all of the abov=
e, with the most time spent on Andreessen=E2=80=99s essays, particularly th=
e recent one on AI, although we talk on some a16z related topics towards th=
e end. As I note in the essay, I personally do generally agree with Andrees=
sen=E2=80=99s view on AI; I do try to push him to flesh out a few of his ar=
guments, but I recognize that those efforts may not be fully satisfactory t=
o some of you. What I do hope is that this interview, like all Stratechery =
content, is very thought-provoking and interesting =E2=80=94 and it is free=
 to share.

 email to add Stratechery to your podcast player.

ineer and I just don=E2=80=99t see an issue here?=E2=80=9D*

*MA:* I would say, again, it=E2=80=99s the constrained vision view of what =
you just said. One way to put it is the positives outweigh the negatives. I=
 don=E2=80=99t expect miracles, I expect basically small tangible steps, an=
d I expect small tangible steps that basically take you forward in good way=
s, and then I expect issues to emerge along the way.

Look, I=E2=80=99ve been dealing with this, the minute we launched the web b=
rowser, people were like, what about cyber crime, and what about this, and =
what about hacking, and personally all this stuff, people stealing credit c=
ard numbers. You know what? That all happened. There=E2=80=99s still ransom=
ware attacks happening over the Internet today, all that stuff is still hap=
pening and whatever other things people don=E2=80=99t like about the Intern=
et, it=E2=80=99s all happening.

Yet at the same time, I think it=E2=80=99s just very clear if you did a soc=
ietal accounting, it=E2=80=99s been a gigantic advance. I think one of the =
tip-offs here is the people that are the angriest about the Internet use th=
e Internet the most. Specifically, they=E2=80=99re angry about the Internet=
 on the Internet.

*It is very true.*

*MA:* They=E2=80=99re making YouTube videos railing against the Internet. T=
hey=E2=80=99re getting big Twitter followings railing against the Internet.

*I mean there=E2=80=99s something about the Internet, I think I had this co=
nversation with a friend, where there is a bit where my view as American cu=
lture is by far the strongest it=E2=80=99s ever been, it=E2=80=99s absolute=
ly dominant; McDonald=E2=80=99s and blue jeans was baby stuff. The reason i=
s because the Internet, and there=E2=80=99s a certain archetype that succee=
ds on the Internet that is very American, it=E2=80=99s very iconoclastic, i=
t=E2=80=99s completely over-the-top, it is a cult-like builder, it=E2=80=99=
s something I=E2=80=99m wary of myself. There=E2=80=99s lots of people that=
 follow me, and they have notifications every tweet that I send, and that=
=E2=80=99s very nerve-wracking or whatever it might be. There=E2=80=99s a b=
it where even the people from other countries that are super dominant in th=
ese, it=E2=80=99s like if you didn=E2=80=99t know what nationalities you th=
ink that person was, and it=E2=80=99s like, yeah, that=E2=80=99s an America=
n. It=E2=80=99s hard to articulate, but I think there is some aspect of tha=
t and it maybe ties back to the, =E2=80=9CAre you still an American company=
?=E2=80=9D question. I don=E2=80=99t know, I=E2=80=99m not sure this is rea=
lly a question, it just prompted that thought in my head.*

*MA:* Yeah, look, there are a lot of people who are mad about that. There a=
re a lot of people in other societies right outside the US where their kids=
, they come from a more traditional culture wherever they are in the world,=
 and their kids are on the Internet and their kids are growing up Americani=
zed. The great book on this is the book on W.E.I.R.D, Joseph Henrich=E2=80=
=99s book called The WEIRDest People in the World ( https://us.macmillan.co=
m/books/9780374710453/theweirdestpeopleintheworld ) where W.E.I.R.D. stands=
 for Western Educated Industrialized Rich and Democratic, which basically i=
s essentially American. Basically he makes the point in the book that the I=
nternet is the global carrier wave for WEIRD, which is to say the internet =
is the global carrier wave for basically Americanism. Look, there are a lot=
 of people in other cultures who are very upset about this and they=E2=80=
=99re on the other side of this. They=E2=80=99re like, =E2=80=9COkay, the k=
ids are not going to follow along in the traditions that we had and the rel=
igion that we had and all the cultural beliefs that we had=E2=80=9D.

*Some people in the US are upset about it.*

*MA:* Exactly, a hundred percent correct. People are going to get mad, are =
they mad for legitimate reasons in a lot of cases? Probably yes. Again, do =
you want to live in a world in which the Internet, as a consequence, do you=
 shut the Internet down? The answer seems quite evidently, no.

To pick up, just real quick on the nuclear power thing, I actually looked u=
p the other day the origin of the concept of the precautionary principle. T=
he precautionary principle is this idea that developers of a technology sho=
uld basically have to prove that it=E2=80=99s not going to be harmful befor=
e it=E2=80=99s released. The origin of the precautionary principle is that =
it was invented by the German Greens in the 1970s, and it was invented to o=
ppose nuclear power.

You fast forward to today and Germany is quite literally in an energy war w=
ith Russia. Germany is funding the Russian war machine by buying energy fro=
m them as is the rest of Europe, then Germany is desperately trying to get =
off of Russian oil and gas, but they will not build nuclear. In fact, becau=
se of the Greens in Germany, they=E2=80=99re shutting down the nuclear powe=
r plants. What Germany is actually doing instead is they=E2=80=99re burning=
 coal. Emissions in Germany are on a rapid rise up because of the use of co=
al, and so you run this again =E2=80=94

*You know Marc, you know how they=E2=80=99re going to get away from coal is=
 by importing US natural gas and becoming that much more dependent on us.*

*MA:* That would be good. That would be a big step forward.

*That=E2=80=99s what=E2=80=99s happening without question.*

*MA:* For them and for us. But again, it=E2=80=99s this thing where, okay, =
the German Greens of the precautionary principle who were so certain that t=
hey had morality figured out, they were so certain that they were going to =
be able to prejudge how these things were going to unfold, they put themsel=
ves and their country in a situation forty years later that=E2=80=99s the e=
xact opposite of what they wanted. It=E2=80=99s just like, all right, these=
 sweeping moral judgements, let=E2=80=99s not do that, let=E2=80=99s be pra=
ctical. Anyway, so that=E2=80=99s why actually I feel quite good about havi=
ng a fundamental engineering grounding, which is when push comes to shove, =
if we can=E2=80=99t predict the future in enormous detail fifty years out, =
we can at least be practical, and that=E2=80=99s what I=E2=80=99m signed up=
 for.

*Well Marc, it was good to have you on, I guess you passed, it was not very=
 VC, so I think it=E2=80=99s all good for Stratechery, but this is actually=
 the last interview that I have for the summer. Will be off next week, whic=
h is perfect because I=E2=80=99m going to be away and if everyone is upset =
and sends me angry emails, I probably won=E2=80=99t see them. Anyhow, it is=
 good to see you and have a good 4th of July. I=E2=80=99ll talk to you soon=
.*

*MA:* Good, awesome. Thank you Ben.
